did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid deep learning model named TransNet, which integrates ResNet-18 and Transformer encoders, to perform multiclass skin lesion classification on the HAM10000 dataset. The study achieved 97.32% classification accuracy and 0.999 ROC-AUC, demonstrating the use of AI/ML for dermatological diagnostic tasks.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new hybrid deep learning model named TransNet (integrating ResNet-18 and Transformer encoders) for skin lesion classification. It details a specific methodology, dataset (HAM10000), training setup, and reports new empirical results (97.32% accuracy, 0.999 ROC-AUC) comparing the proposed model against benchmarks. Although it includes a literature review section, the primary contribution is the development and validation of a new algorithm, classifying it as a research paper. It was published in the 2025 6th International Conference on Innovative Computing (ICIC).

title: Skin Lesion Classification Using a Hybrid ResNet– Transformer Encoder Architecture
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of precise skin lesion classification for early melanoma detection by proposing TransNet, a hybrid deep learning model that integrates ResNet-18 and Transformer encoders. The model was trained and evaluated on the multiclass HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 dermatoscopic images across seven diagnostic categories. To handle class imbalance, the authors employed balanced training techniques and standardized preprocessing, including image resizing to 224x224 pixels and ImageNet normalization. The main evaluation approach involved comparing TransNet against benchmark models such as VGG16, ResNet-50, and Vision Transformer using accuracy, F1-score, and ROC-AUC metrics. TransNet achieved a classification accuracy of 97.32% and an ROC-AUC of 0.999, outperforming standard CNNs and demonstrating superior efficiency with only 14 million parameters. The authors conclude that TransNet offers an optimal balance of accuracy and computational cost, making it suitable for clinical and mobile deployment.

lesion_types_studied.details: Majority class in HAM10000 dataset with 6,705 samples (67%); down-sampled during balancing.
Malignant class in HAM10000 dataset with 1,113 samples (11%); down-sampled during balancing.
Benign class in HAM10000 dataset with 1,099 samples (11%); down-sampled during balancing.
Minority class in HAM10000 dataset with 514 samples (5.1%); up-sampled via augmentation.
Minority class in HAM10000 dataset with 327 samples (3.3%); up-sampled via augmentation.
Minority class in HAM10000 dataset with 142 samples (1.4%); up-sampled via augmentation.
Minority class in HAM10000 dataset with 115 samples (1.1%); up-sampled via augmentation.

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Melanocytic nevi, Melanoma, Benign keratosis, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratoses, Vascular lesions, Dermatofibroma)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (7 classes) using a linear classification layer with softmax activation to provide probability distributions among classes. Performance was evaluated using accuracy (97.32%), precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC (0.999).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, gathered from various populations and using multiple modalities (contact polarized, contact non-polarized, non-contact polarized) at the Department of Dermatology of the Medical University of Vienna in partnership with foreign institutions. It is publicly available via Harvard Dataverse and the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Archive.

use.training: Used for training the TransNet model. The dataset was balanced by down-sampling majority classes and augmenting minority classes, resulting in a training set of 3,759 images (approximately 537 per class).
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate held-out validation set; the paper mentions maintaining realistic evaluation conditions but does not specify a validation split count.
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation of the final model performance (accuracy and ROC-AUC).

statistical_methods_used.method: ROC-AUC (Receiver Operating Characteristic - Area Under Curve)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the model's ability to distinguish between classes and assess overall diagnostic performance across all diagnostic categories.
To measure classification performance, providing a balanced view of performance across majority and minority classes.
To balance class distribution in the training set (threshold of 500 samples per class) and maintain realistic evaluation conditions in the test set.
To ensure deterministic results and reproducibility for Python, NumPy, and PyTorch operations.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between TransNet and benchmark models were based on descriptive metrics (Accuracy, F1-Score, ROC-AUC) without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: TransNet achieved 97.32% test accuracy and 0.999 ROC-AUC. While Swin Transformer and Inception-v3 showed higher accuracy (99.70% and 99.21% respectively) and perfect ROC-AUC (1.000), TransNet was noted for having fewer parameters (14M) and better efficiency. Class-wise ROC-AUC for TransNet ranged from 0.87 (BKL) to 1.00 (AKIEC, BCC, MEL, NV, VASC, DF). The test set contained 1,057 images with original class distribution.

architecture_details.tokenization: Spatial to Sequential Conversion: 512x7x7 feature map converted to 49 tokens of 512-D vectors, then reduced to 96-D

transformer_encoder.attention_mechanism: Multi-Head Self-Attention with 8 heads (12-D dimensions each, total 96-D)
transformer_encoder.attention_type: Scaled dot-product attention with softmax normalization
transformer_encoder.normalization: Layer Normalization applied to every sub-layer (pre-norm architecture)
transformer_encoder.residual_connections: Skip connections on both attention and FFN sub-layers

architecture_details.pooling: Global mean pooling over the 49 token sequence dimension to produce a 96-dimensional feature vector

training_details.learning_rate: Initial 1×10⁻⁴ with ReduceLROnPlateau (factor=0.5, patience=5 epochs, min=1×10⁻⁷)
training_details.weight_decay: L2 regularization with coefficient 1×10⁻⁴ (applied to all learnable parameters except batch/layer normalization)
training_details.hardware: NVIDIA GPU with 16GB memory, 32GB system RAM
training_details.random_seeds: Fixed random seeds (42) for Python, NumPy, PyTorch

preprocessing_techniques: Image Resizing: All images resized to 224x224 pixels using bicubic interpolation to match ResNet-18 input size.
Normalization: Pixel values normalized to [0,1] range by division by 255, then standardized using ImageNet statistics (mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]) across RGB channels.
Color Space Preservation: Images maintained in RGB color space as color data is essential for diagnosis.

Data Balancing: Threshold of 500 samples per class applied; down-sampling of majority classes (NV, MEL, BKL) via random selection; up-sampling of minority classes (AKIEC, BCC, VASC, DF) via augmentation.
Geometric Augmentation: Rotation (±30°), Flips (50% chance), Scaling (0.8x–1.2x), and Cropping (224x224 from 256x256) applied to minority classes.
Photometric Augmentation: Brightness adjustment (±20%), Contrast adjustment (±15%), Color Jitter (Minor), and Gaussian Noise (σ=0.01) applied to minority classes.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to minority classes (AKIEC, BCC, VASC, DF). Techniques included: Rotation (±30°), Flips (50% chance), Scaling (0.8x–1.2x), and Cropping (224x224 from 256x256).
Applied to minority classes. Techniques included: Brightness adjustment (±20%), Contrast adjustment (±15%), Color Jitter (Minor), and Gaussian Noise (σ=0.01).

Threshold of 500 samples per class was set. Down-sampling was applied to majority classes (NV, MEL, BKL) using random selection. Up-sampling via augmentation was applied to minority classes (AKIEC, BCC, VASC, DF). Resulted in a training set of 3,759 images (approx. 537 per class).

conclusions: The proposed hybrid model, TransNet, which integrates ResNet-18 and Transformer encoders, achieved a classification accuracy of 97.32% and an ROC-AUC of 0.999 on the HAM10000 dataset.

TransNet outperforms standard Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) such as VGG16 and ResNet-50, as well as Vision Transformer (ViT), in terms of the balance between accuracy and computational efficiency.

While Swin Transformer and Inception-v3 demonstrated slightly higher testing accuracy (99.70% and 99.21% respectively) and perfect ROC-AUC (1.000), TransNet is considered more practical for clinical and mobile deployment due to its lower computational cost and smaller parameter count (14 million).

The study addresses the challenge of class imbalance in skin lesion classification by employing balanced training techniques (down-sampling majority classes and augmenting minority classes), allowing the model to maintain realistic evaluation conditions on the test set.

The hybrid architecture effectively combines local feature extraction from CNNs with global context modeling from Transformers, offering a solution to the limitations of using either architecture alone (CNNs ignoring broader context and Transformers requiring large datasets/computation).
TransNet offers an optimal equilibrium between accuracy and efficiency, making it suitable for real-world clinical settings and mobile applications where computational resources may be limited.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which, while multi-source, consists of dermoscopic images; the authors explicitly note that the model may not generalize well to clinical photographs or other imaging modalities.
No external validation was performed; the model was trained and tested only on the HAM10000 dataset, limiting the assessment of real-world clinical robustness.

Class imbalance was addressed via aggressive balancing techniques (down-sampling majority classes and augmenting minority classes) during training, resulting in a training set of 3,759 images; however, the test set retained the original imbalanced distribution (1,057 images), which may overestimate performance on rare classes in unbalanced real-world scenarios.

The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the comparison between TransNet and benchmark models; performance differences are based solely on descriptive metrics.

While TransNet achieved high accuracy (97.32%), benchmark models like Swin Transformer and Inception-v3 reported higher testing accuracy (99.70% and 99.21% respectively) and perfect ROC-AUC (1.000), suggesting TransNet's superiority is primarily in efficiency (14M parameters) rather than absolute peak accuracy.

The dataset contains significant heterogeneity in image acquisition (different dermatoscopic instruments, populations, and archival systems), which is a strength for generalizability but also a source of noise that the model must handle.
The paper acknowledges that rare classes might exhibit subtle representation problems despite augmentation, and performance may drop on lesions not present in the HAM10000 dataset.
The study is a conference paper published in 2025, which may indicate a shorter review cycle and less extensive validation compared to journal publications.
